title: Disease-modifying interactions between chronic kidney disease and osteoarthritis: a new comorbid mouse model
research_question: What are the disease-modifying interactions between chronic kidney disease (CKD) and osteoarthritis (OA) in a comorbid mouse model?
methods_summary: Male C57BL/6 mice underwent 5/6 nephrectomy (5/6Nx) to induce CKD, with or without destabilisation of medial meniscus (DMM) to induce OA, or sham surgeries.

OA pathology and CKD were assessed 12 weeks post-induction using blinded histology scoring, micro-CT, immunohistochemistry for osteoclast and MMP-13 activity, and serum analysis of bone metabolic markers.
dataset: 70 male C57BL/6 mice (8-10 weeks old) divided into four groups: sham, 5/6Nx-sham, DMM, and 5/6Nx-DMM.
key_findings: 5/6Nx alone did not induce cartilage pathology but increased synovial MMP-13 expression and subchondral bone osteoclastic activity, reducing subchondral trabecular bone volume and mineral density.
DMM alone induced typical OA pathology (cartilage damage, subchondral bone sclerosis, osteophytes, synovitis) and also caused renal fibrosis and proteinuria.
5/6Nx significantly reduced DMM-induced cartilage pathology and osteophyte formation, associated with reduced subchondral bone volume and density and inhibition of osteoclast activation.
Bidirectional disease-modifying interactions were observed: CKD altered OA bone remodeling, and OA affected renal morphology and function.
extracted_insights: This first preclinical comorbid CKD-OA model demonstrates significant bidirectional disease-modifying interactions, with CKD reducing OA severity via altered subchondral bone remodeling, while OA induces renal pathology, highlighting potential cross-talk mechanisms.
